root_text.an10.111:1.1: Atha kho aññataro bhikkhu yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho so bhikkhu bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.an10.111:2.3: “Idha, bhikkhu, bhikkhu asekhāya sammādiṭṭhiyā samannāgato hoti, asekhena sammāsaṅkappena samannāgato hoti, asekhāya sammāvācāya samannāgato hoti, asekhena sammākammantena samannāgato hoti, asekhena sammāājīvena samannāgato hoti, asekhena sammāvāyāmena samannāgato hoti, asekhāya sammāsatiyā samannāgato hoti, asekhena sammāsamādhinā samannāgato hoti, asekhena sammāñāṇena samannāgato hoti, asekhāya sammāvimuttiyā samannāgato hoti.
root_text.an10.111:2.4: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhu, bhikkhu asekho hotī”ti.

translation_text.an10.111:1.1: Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him:
translation_text.an10.111:2.1: “Sir, they speak of the one called ‘an adept’.
translation_text.an10.111:2.2: How is an adept mendicant defined?”
translation_text.an10.111:2.3: “Mendicant, it’s when a mendicant has an adept’s right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right immersion, right knowledge, and right freedom.
translation_text.an10.111:2.4: That’s how a mendicant is an adept.”
